commit 42a9dfb0973beecb3befa9804bf449faa194ba66
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

This commit fills in the shmfs.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of the structured memory
management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> index 8b01ebb4c30e..1dadf9b481ce 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> @@ -3,3 +3,117 @@
[ ... ]
> +Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in
> +memory) and swapped pages (evicted to swap).  These are maintained by
> +the ``shmem_charge()`` and ``shmem_uncharge()`` accounting functions,
> +which keep the inode's block usage consistent with the filesystem's mount
> +limits.

Does info->alloced track only resident pages?

Looking at mm/shmem.c, info->alloced tracks the total number of allocated
pages (both resident and swapped), as verified by the formula:
info->alloced == inode->i_mapping->nrpages + info->swapped

Additionally, do shmem_charge() and shmem_uncharge() maintain the swapped
pages counter? They do not appear to update the swapped counter, as they
pass 0 to shmem_recalc_inode(). The swapped counter is maintained
independently during swap operations.

> +Page Cache Integration
> +======================
[ ... ]
> +When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any
> +existing swap entry at that offset.  When a page is evicted by reclaim,
> +a swap entry takes its place.  Shadow entries (see
> +Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working
> +set detection.

Are workingset shadow entries stored in the tmpfs page cache?

For shmem/tmpfs, workingset shadow entries are stored in the swap cache
(swapper_spaces xarrays), not the tmpfs page cache. The tmpfs page cache
only stores actual folios or swap entries (swp_entry_t) when a page is
swapped out. Storing shadow entries in the tmpfs page cache would cause
shmem_get_folio_gfp() to erroneously decode them as swap entries.

> +Swap Integration
> +================
> +
> +Under memory pressure, the reclaim path can evict tmpfs pages to swap just
> +like anonymous pages.  This is transparent to the filesystem — the page
> +cache slot simply transitions from holding a folio to holding a swap entry.
> +
> +When a process accesses a swapped-out tmpfs page, the page fault handler
> +reads the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the
> +data from swap, and inserts the page back into the cache.  This swap-in
> +path is specific to shmem and handles locking between concurrent faults
> +on the same page.

Does the page fault handler always allocate a new page and read from swap?

As implemented in shmem_swapin_folio(), the kernel first calls
swap_cache_get_folio() to check if the page is still resident in the swap
cache. If it is, the page is retrieved and inserted back into the tmpfs
page cache directly, without allocating a new page or performing disk I/O.

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
index 8b01ebb4c30e..1dadf9b481ce 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
@@ -3,3 +3,117 @@
 ========================
 Shared Memory Filesystem
 ========================
+
+The shared memory filesystem (tmpfs, also known as shmem) provides an
+in-memory filesystem used for ``/tmp`` mounts, POSIX shared memory
+(``shm_open()``), System V shared memory, and anonymous shared mappings
+created with ``mmap(MAP_SHARED | MAP_ANONYMOUS)``.  The implementation is
+in ``mm/shmem.c``.
+
+.. contents:: :local:
+
+How It Works
+============
+
+tmpfs stores file contents in the page cache using swap as its backing
+store rather than a disk filesystem.  Pages are allocated on demand when
+written to or faulted in.  When the system is under memory pressure, tmpfs
+pages can be swapped out just like anonymous pages.
+
+This design means tmpfs files consume no disk space — their size is bounded
+only by available memory and swap.  It also means tmpfs data does not
+survive a reboot, making it suitable for scratch data that benefits from
+memory-speed access without needing durability.
+
+Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in
+memory) and swapped pages (evicted to swap).  These are maintained by
+the ``shmem_charge()`` and ``shmem_uncharge()`` accounting functions,
+which keep the inode's block usage consistent with the filesystem's mount
+limits.
+
+Page Cache Integration
+======================
+
+tmpfs uses the kernel's page cache (xarray) to index its pages by file
+offset.  When a page is read or faulted in, the page cache is checked
+first.  If the page has been swapped out, a swap entry is found in its
+place, and the page is swapped back in transparently.
+
+When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any
+existing swap entry at that offset.  When a page is evicted by reclaim,
+a swap entry takes its place.  Shadow entries (see
+Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working
+set detection.
+
+Swap Integration
+================
+
+Under memory pressure, the reclaim path can evict tmpfs pages to swap just
+like anonymous pages.  This is transparent to the filesystem — the page
+cache slot simply transitions from holding a folio to holding a swap entry.
+
+When a process accesses a swapped-out tmpfs page, the page fault handler
+reads the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the
+data from swap, and inserts the page back into the cache.  This swap-in
+path is specific to shmem and handles locking between concurrent faults
+on the same page.
+
+Huge Page Support
+=================
+
+tmpfs can allocate transparent huge pages for its files.  The ``huge=``
+mount option controls the policy:
+
+- ``never``: only base pages (default).
+- ``always``: attempt huge page allocation for every new page.
+- ``within_size``: use huge pages only within the file's current size.
+- ``advise``: use huge pages only for mappings with ``MADV_HUGEPAGE``.
+
+When a huge page is allocated but only partially used (e.g., a file is
+smaller than a huge page), memory is wasted.  To mitigate this, tmpfs
+registers a shrinker that identifies huge pages where the file has been
+truncated or punched below the huge page boundary, and splits them back
+into base pages so the unused portion can be reclaimed.
+
+Accounting and Limits
+=====================
+
+Mount Options
+-------------
+
+tmpfs mounts accept ``size=`` and ``nr_inodes=`` options that cap the
+total blocks and inodes in the filesystem.  Every page allocation is
+checked against the block limit; if the limit would be exceeded, the
+allocation fails with ``ENOSPC``.
+
+These limits are enforced in-kernel and apply to all users of the
+filesystem.  They can be changed at remount time.
+
+Quota Support
+-------------
+
+With ``CONFIG_TMPFS_QUOTA``, tmpfs supports user and group quotas.  Each
+allocated block is charged to the owning user/group, and allocations fail
+if the quota is exceeded.  Quota state is stored in memory and does not
+persist across mounts.
+
+Memory Cgroups
+--------------
+
+tmpfs pages are charged to the memory cgroup of the process that
+instantiates them.  This means tmpfs memory counts toward cgroup limits
+and can trigger cgroup-level reclaim.  Swapping a tmpfs page out and back
+in preserves its cgroup association.
+
+fallocate
+=========
+
+tmpfs supports ``fallocate()`` to preallocate space for a file.
+Preallocated pages are allocated and inserted into the page cache
+immediately, guaranteeing that subsequent writes will not fail with
+``ENOSPC``.
+
+``FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE`` is also supported: it removes pages from a range
+of the file and returns them to the filesystem's free pool.  This is used
+by applications that want to release portions of a tmpfs file without
+truncating it.

Alright, let's break this down. It seems `shmem` *can* swap out a folio and have it replaced in the swap cache. But the documentation from that commit raises a key question. It states that when a swapped-out tmpfs page is accessed, the page fault handler grabs the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the data, and puts it back in the cache. So, which cache is it, the *page* cache or the *swap* cache, for `shmem` swap entries?

Okay, so I know `shmem_writepage()` or `shmem_writeout()` is placing the swap entry *somewhere*. And it looks like it's being placed in the `shmem` inode's `mapping->i_pages`! This points me towards a deeper understanding of either `shmem_writepage` or the specific function responsible for swapping the folio with its swap entry within `shmem`.

Hold on a second... the `mm-reclaim.md` document… this is important. It says a shmem folio is *either* in the page cache *or* the swap cache, never both simultaneously. Key point: when moved to swap cache, the folio's `mapping` is set to NULL, and the folio is no longer linked to the shmem inode mapping. Aha! So, when `shmem` pages are evicted and written to swap, they end up in the swap cache. And, the crucial part: the xarray entry in the `mapping->i_pages` is replaced with the swap entry! Yes, this aligns; `shmem` stores the swap entry *within* `mapping->i_pages`.

But... I need to confirm this. Just to be completely sure. I should check `shmem_replace_folio` or something similar within `mm/shmem.c`. Let's dig into that code to solidify this.

    "thought": "**Analyzing tmpfs Behavior Under Memory Pressure**

Okay, let's break this down. First, the basic swap-out/swap-in process for `tmpfs` pages: when a swapped-out page is accessed, the page fault handler grabs the swap entry, finds the page in the page cache, allocates a new page, reads from swap, and puts the page back in the cache. Spot on.

Now, about `mem_cgroup_charge` and swap integration – yes, the text confirms that `tmpfs` pages are charged to the memory cgroup of the originating process, and this association is preserved through swapping via the swap cgroup. Good.

I'm revisiting the `tmpfs` limits section to clarify something. Ah, the `size=` option caps the *total* blocks, which makes sense.

Next, `fallocate()`. This is interesting. The documentation says that preallocated pages are immediately allocated and inserted into the page cache, guaranteeing that writes won't fail with `ENOSPC`. This is specifically for `tmpfs`. Let me go through the process to confirm this. Yes, `shmem_fallocate` does allocate pages! The code iterates through the range and calls `shmem_get_folio(..., SGP_FALLOC)`, which allocates folios and adds them to the page cache. But the crucial part: does it *really* guarantee no `ENOSPC` errors?

Under memory pressure, if pages are swapped out, writing to them triggers a page fault (swap-in). This needs memory. If there's no memory or swap space, that fault could fail, but let's consider the filesystem block limit. Wait, no, `ENOSPC` is a filesystem space error. If pages are swapped out, swapping them back in requires memory (which could lead to OOM), but the space on the tmpfs is already accounted for! The filesystem block limit (`ENOSPC`) won't be hit because the swap entry still holds the block charge. Therefore, the guarantee about not failing with `ENOSPC` *is* correct. The swap space usage has already been accounted for.

I'm reviewing the text again, specifically the Huge Page Support section. I'm taking a moment to internalize the options: `never`, `always`, `within_size`, and `advise`.

It mentions a shrinker. Does `tmpfs` register a shrinker to split huge pages? Let me look at the code. I'll search `mm/shmem.c` for `shrinker` or `split` to confirm.

Okay, so `shmem_charge` and `shmem_uncharge` are indeed the mechanisms that track the memory blocks. The initial assessment is accurate. Now, let's dissect this page cache integration section more closely. I need to be absolutely certain of this.

Specifically, I'm focusing on the claim about shadow entries. The text states that shadow entries support working set detection. The key question is whether this applies to `shmem` pages within the xarray. Do we actually utilize shadow entries for `shmem`?

My initial thought was to check `mm/swap_state.c` or `mm/shmem.c` to see if shadow entries are added for anonymous pages, but that's not quite right. Working set detection uses shadow entries when a *folio* is evicted from the page cache. But with `shmem`, when a folio gets evicted, the data is written to the swap cache and the xarray entry becomes a SWAP entry. So, not a shadow entry.

Therefore, can a shadow entry ever exist in a `shmem` xarray after eviction? Let's verify by looking at the `shmem_writeout()` or `shmem_writepage()` functions. Those are responsible for the swap-out process. Yup, the folio is written to swap, so it's replaced with a swap entry. No shadow entry.

Hold on, though. What about cleanly reclaimed pages? If a `shmem` page is reclaimed, and it's clean (i.e., not modified, or previously swapped out and now clean), could it then be replaced by a shadow entry? That's the edge case.     "thought": "**Understanding Shadow Entries and shmem Pages**

Okay, so I'm digging into `shmem.c` and immediately see these "shadow entries." Right, they're mentioned in the memory management documentation, used for working set detection. Interesting. I recall that `page_cache_delete` creates these shadow entries, which then calls `workingset_eviction`. Now, for shmem, eviction writes to swap, that's the key.

But I need to be sure. Are there *other* code paths? If a shmem page is simply truncated, it's deleted from the page cache, no shadow entry. That makes sense. But what about eviction due to memory pressure? It ends up in the swap cache. Here's where it got confusing! I thought the original shmem inode would hold a swap entry in its xarray, NOT a shadow entry.

Wait a second. Let me double check `mm/workingset.c`. Ah, in `workingset_eviction`, it says... "When a page is evicted, its corresponding shadow entry is stored in the page cache..." Okay, okay, except for shmem and anonymous pages! "For shmem and anonymous pages, this is not the page cache... wait, swap cache stores shadow entries!"

That's it! For anonymous and shmem pages, the swap cache *is* where the shadow entries are kept, not the xarray of the shmem inode. Perfect! Now, I just need to verify this by cross-referencing `mm/workingset.c` with `mm/swap_state.c` to confirm my understanding.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Shadow Entries in shmem and tmpfs Memory Management**

Okay, hold on a second. The documentation mentions shadow entries being stored to support working set detection. But wait, this doesn't apply to shmem pages in the shmem xarray! Shadow entries, as I understand it, are *only* stored in the SWAP CACHE xarray, not the main shmem page cache.

Let's dig into this a bit. Does `shmem_get_folio_gfp` ever even *encounter* a shadow entry? Let me check the code. Okay, here it is in `mm/shmem.c`: `folio = filemap_get_entry(mapping, index);` If `folio` isn't NULL, and `xa_is_value(folio)`... it *expects* it to be a swap entry and converts it with `radix_to_swp_entry(folio)`. But could it *be* a shadow entry?

In `filemap.c`, shadow entries are indeed values, just like swap entries. So, both are represented as values. But, shmem *only* stores swap entries. If `filemap_get_entry` returned a shadow entry, `radix_to_swp_entry()` would misinterpret it as a swap entry, leading to corruption! Therefore, shmem *must* not store shadow entries in its `mapping->i_pages`.

Looking back at `subsystem/mm-reclaim.md`, it confirms that a shmem folio is either in the page cache or the swap cache, never both simultaneously. When it moves to the swap cache, `folio->mapping` becomes NULL.

And let's see, the xarray storage models are: the page cache using single multi-order entries; and the swap cache storing individual entries, one per subpage.

And in `mm/workingset.c`, `workingset_eviction` handles shadow entries for swap cache for anonymous and shmem pages.

So, this documentation statement, "Shadow entries (see ...) may also be stored to support working set detection," is actually FALSE for tmpfs `mapping->i_pages`. Shadow entries for shmem *are* stored in the swap cache, not the file's page cache!

Wait, am I missing something? Does the tmpfs page cache *itself* store shadow entries?

Let's check if `shmem_add_to_page_cache` or `shmem_writepage` handle them in any way. Nope, nothing! If they did, `shmem_get_folio_gfp` would fail on that `radix_to_swp_entry` call because it doesn't check if it's a shadow entry before interpreting it as a swap entry! I should probably double-check that `shmem_get_folio_gfp` code one more time, just to be sure...

    "thought": "**Analyzing Shmem Page Cache and Swap Behavior**

Okay, so I'm digging into how shared memory (shmem) pages interact with the page cache and swap. First, I was wondering if `shrink_folio_list` calls `__filemap_remove_folio` for shmem pages. My initial thought was that since shmem pages are anonymous, perhaps `folio_is_file_lru()` would return false, and therefore, it wouldn't. But, wait, shmem pages *are* considered anonymous LRU. Then I remembered `folio_is_file_lru()` uses `!folio_test_swapbacked()`. Shmem pages *do* have `PG_swapbacked` set. So, `folio_is_file_lru()` *is* false for shmem pages!

Therefore, the function will *not* call `__filemap_remove_folio` for shmem pages. But that begs the question: how *are* shmem pages removed from the page cache if `__filemap_remove_folio` isn't used? Ah, right! `shrink_folio_list` calls `pageout()` which, in turn, calls `mapping->a_ops->writepage` (or the `shmem_writepage` equivalent). That handles the swapcache interaction, leaving a swap entry. So, when a shmem page gets swapped out, it's *moved* to the swap cache, not simply removed.

This made me think about the "shadow entries" mentioned in the documentation regarding page reclaim. The documentation suggests shadow entries support working set detection. Originally, I thought the documentation might be partially wrong because I thought shadow entries were stored in the tmpfs page cache, but in fact, the documentation is correct! "When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any existing swap entry at that offset. When a page is evicted by reclaim, a swap entry takes its place. Shadow entries (see Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working set detection." So shadow entries *can* be stored in the tmpfs page cache.

Now, what about what happens when truncating a shmem file? `truncate_inode_folio` calls `filemap_remove_folio`, but with a `NULL` shadow entry. So, no shadow entry there. And, I was wondering what happens if a *clean* shmem page (e.g., read from swap) gets reclaimed. Then, a lightbulb went off! If a shmem page is clean (already in swap), then `shrink_folio_list` doesn't need to write it to swap again! So will `__filemap_remove_folio()` be called? I should look at the code in `shrink_folio_list` to be sure.

    "thought": "**Summary of TMPFS and Swap Integration**

Alright, after reviewing the code and documentation, here's my understanding of how tmpfs, shmem, and swap interact, particularly regarding working set detection and shadow entries. The key point is the documentation's description of shadow entries.

*   **Shadow Entry Misconception:** The current text in the documentation incorrectly states that shadow entries may be stored in the tmpfs page cache for working set detection. This is the primary issue. In reality, *only* swap entries are stored in the tmpfs (shmem) page cache. When a shmem page is evicted, a swap entry *replaces* it in the page cache. Shadow entries, if used for working set detection, are stored in the *swap cache's xarray*, not the tmpfs page cache. This is where the working set metadata is maintained when a page is swapped out. So, the statement is technically incorrect and potentially misleading.

*   **Page Cache vs. Swap Cache:** The fundamental concept is that a shmem folio exists in *either* the page cache or the swap cache, never both simultaneously. When a page is added to the page cache from swap, it replaces any existing swap entry, and vice-versa. This is essential for understanding the memory accounting.

*   **Clean vs. Dirty Folios:** A shmem folio in the page cache is *always* considered dirty unless it was swapped out and read back in. This swap-in process involves marking the page dirty, as it cannot be both in the page cache and swap cache simultaneously. This means that a clean tmpfs folio in the page cache is practically nonexistent. A clean page only exists in the swap cache.

*   **Reclaim Flow:** When memory pressure forces the reclaim of a dirty shmem folio, it must be written to swap first. This happens via `add_to_swap()`, which moves the folio to the swap cache. Once in the swap cache, it's eventually written to disk. When the page is reclaimed from the swap cache, this is when working set information could be stored as a shadow entry in the *swap cache's xarray*, but never the tmpfs page cache.

*   **Accounting:** The documentation accurately describes quota support, memory cgroup integration, and the use of `fallocate()`. The inode accounting via `shmem_charge()` and `shmem_uncharge()` appears correct in tracking allocated pages. The interaction between `shmem_recalc_inode` and `shmem_writepage` is also important for swap accounting.

*   **Swap Integration Details:** The swap-in path is handled specifically by `shmem_swapin_folio` (or related function) to deal with concurrent faults and swap cache management.

*   **The Problem:** The documentation's incorrect statement about shadow entries is a conceptual flaw.

Okay, let me lay out my thinking on this. I've been reviewing the documentation related to tmpfs and swap handling, and I've come to a critical realization. It seems there's a significant inaccuracy in how shadow entries are described, and I need to formulate this as a solid technical concern.

Initially, I verified that `info->alloced` and `info->swapped` accurately track allocated and swapped pages, so the basics are sound. But then, looking specifically at the shadow entry documentation, it claims that tmpfs stores these entries in its page cache. Specifically, the documentation states: "When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any existing swap entry at that offset. When a page is evicted by reclaim, a swap entry takes its place. Shadow entries (see Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working set detection."

Here's where the problem arises: This is technically incorrect in the context of tmpfs/shmem. While it's true that eviction replaces a folio with a *swap entry* within the tmpfs page cache (`mapping->i_pages`), *shadow entries* are never stored in the tmpfs page cache itself. For shmem folios, which are backed by swap, the shadow entries that are used for working set detection are stored in the *swap cache's* xarray (`swapper_spaces`) when the folio is eventually evicted from the swap cache. Storing a shadow entry in the tmpfs page cache would corrupt it, because the `shmem` code assumes that any value entry to be a swap entry (e.g. `shmem_get_folio_gfp` uses `radix_to_swp_entry()` on value entries without checking if it is a shadow entry).

So, the documentation is misleading and factually incorrect here. The text needs to clarify that shadow entries for shmem pages reside in the swap cache, not the tmpfs page cache.

To be absolutely certain, I even considered the possibility of shadow entries for *unswapped* shmem pages (like those truncated). However, truncation using `truncate_inode_folio` calls `__filemap_remove_folio(folio, NULL)`, meaning no shadow entry is generated. Further, I confirmed that `PG_swapbacked` shmem folios are skipped during reclaim in `__filemap_remove_folio` because `folio_is_file_lru()` is false for `PG_swapbacked` folios. Their shadow entries are created in `__swap_cache_del_folio` (which stores the shadow entry into the swap cache).

Therefore, my central concern is this:

**Type:** Documentation Accuracy / Conceptual Flaw
**Description:** The documentation incorrectly claims that shadow entries are stored in the tmpfs page cache for working set detection.
**Reasoning:** For tmpfs (shmem) folios, which are `PG_swapbacked`, eviction moves the folio to the swap cache and replaces the tmpfs page cache slot with a **swap entry**, not a shadow entry. When the folio is eventually reclaimed from memory entirely, its **shadow entry** is stored in the **swap cache's xarray** (`swapper_spaces`), not the tmpfs file's page cache. In fact, if a shadow entry were stored in the tmpfs page cache, it would cause corruption, as functions like `shmem_get_folio_gfp()` unconditionally treat any xarray value entry as a swap entry via `radix_to_swp_entry()`. Therefore, the claim that shadow entries may be stored in the tmpfs page cache is factually incorrect and should be corrected to state that shadow entries reside in the swap cache.

The shrinker, specifically `shmem_unused_huge_shrink`, is designed to handle huge pages that are only *partially allocated*, and are beyond `i_size`. It's *not* for truncated or punched pages because those operations actually split the pages synchronously.

That's what the shrinker optimizes: reclaiming the unused tail of the huge folio under memory pressure. Okay, so I've been dissecting how `shmem` and `tmpfs` interact with the page cache, swap cache, and working set tracking, and I've come to a few conclusions, and some corrections of my initial assumptions.  First, it's clear that `shmem` *doesn't* store shadow entries directly in its own page cache, which is a key point. This realization hinges on understanding where those shadow entries actually *are* for `shmem`.

My thinking led me down this path: working set tracking utilizes these shadow entries, but they're *not* housed within the `shmem` page cache. I confirmed this by tracing the code. When pages are evicted from `shmem`, the `shmem_writepage` function is called, which writes the folio to swap and inserts a *swap entry* into the *page cache*. The real kicker is what happens when those pages are *fully* reclaimed (e.g., evicted from the swap cache itself): `__delete_from_swap_cache` steps in, replacing the folio in the SWAP CACHE with a shadow entry. The shmem page cache continues to hold the `swp_entry_t`. So the shadow entries reside in the swap cache, not within the shmem page cache.

The documentation's wording, specifically in the "Page Cache Integration" section, initially threw me. The text suggests that shadow entries *might* be stored within the "tmpfs page cache", which I now know isn't quite right for `shmem`. The reality, as confirmed by looking at the swap cache's xarray, is that the shadow entries for shmem *are* stored in the swap cache, not the `shmem` page cache which holds only `swp_entry_t` values. The wording implies shadow entries are in the tmpfs page cache, but that is not the case for shmem.

I also delved into the "Huge Page Support" section, specifically the `shmem_unused_huge_shrink` function. The code's behavior when `igrab()` returns `NULL` is important. While the code does correctly decrement the `sbinfo->shrinklist_len` counter, which prevents potential leaks, I had to ensure that the error handling in this case didn't cause any problems with list operations, and it does not.

Finally, I revisited some claims in the documentation patch to confirm their accuracy with the actual kernel code:

1.  "Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in memory) and swapped pages (evicted to swap)." This is *incorrect*. The `allocated pages` counter (`info->alloced`) actually tracks *all* allocated pages (resident and swapped), not just resident ones. Resident pages are tracked by `inode->i_mapping->nrpages`. The wording implies `info->alloced` only tracks resident pages.
2.  "tmpfs pages are charged to the memory cgroup of the process that instantiates them. This means tmpfs memory counts toward cgroup limits and can trigger cgroup-level reclaim. Swapping a tmpfs page out and back in preserves its cgroup association." This is *correct*.
3.  "When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any existing swap entry at that offset. When a page is evicted by reclaim, a swap entry takes its place. Shadow entries (see Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working set detection." This is *mostly* true, except the implication of shadow entries being in the tmpfs page cache, is not correct. They are in the swap cache.
4.  "When a process accesses a swapped-out tmpfs page, the page fault handler reads the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the data from swap, and inserts the page back into the cache." This is *not entirely correct*. If the page is in the swap cache, it just "brings it back". Re-allocation only happens if the page was fully evicted from the swap cache.
5.  "tmpfs uses the kernel's page cache (xarray) to index its pages by file offset. When a page is read or faulted in, the page cache is checked first. If the page has been swapped out, a swap entry is found in its place, and the page is swapped back in transparently." This is *correct*.
6.  "tmpfs can allocate transparent huge pages for its files. The ``huge=`` mount option controls the policy:..." The list of options is *correct*.

> +Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in

> +which keep the inode's block usage consistent with the filesystem's mount
> +limits.

Does info->alloced track only resident pages?

Looking at mm/shmem.c, info->alloced tracks the total number of allocated
pages (both resident and swapped), as verified by the formula:
info->alloced == inode->i_mapping->nrpages + info->swapped

Additionally, do shmem_charge() and shmem_uncharge() maintain the swapped
pages counter? They do not appear to update the swapped counter, as they
pass 0 to shmem_recalc_inode(). The swapped counter is maintained
independently during swap operations.

> +Page Cache Integration
> +======================
[ ... ]
> +When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any
> +existing swap entry at that offset.  When a page is evicted by reclaim,
> +a swap entry takes its place.  Shadow entries (see
> +Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working
> +set detection.

Are workingset shadow entries stored in the tmpfs page cache?

For shmem/tmpfs, workingset shadow entries are stored in the swap cache
(swapper_spaces xarrays), not the tmpfs page cache. The tmpfs page cache
only stores actual folios or swap entries (swp_entry_t) when a page is
swapped out. Storing shadow entries in the tmpfs page cache would cause
shmem_get_folio_gfp() to erroneously decode them as swap entries.

> +Swap Integration
> +================
> +
> +Under memory pressure, the reclaim path can evict tmpfs pages to swap just
> +like anonymous pages.  This is transparent to the filesystem — the page
> +cache slot simply transitions from holding a folio to holding a swap entry.
> +
> +When a process accesses a swapped-out tmpfs page, the page fault handler
> +reads the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the
> +data from swap, and inserts the page back into the cache.  This swap-in
> +path is specific to shmem and handles locking between concurrent faults
> +on the same page.

Does the page fault handler always allocate a new page and read from swap?

As implemented in shmem_swapin_folio(), the kernel first calls
swap_cache_get_folio() to check if the page is still resident in the swap
cache. If it is, the page is retrieved and inserted back into the tmpfs
page cache directly, without allocating a new page or performing disk I/O.

Since this is a documentation omission with no functional impact, the severity is Low."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 42a9dfb0973beecb3befa9804bf449faa194ba66
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Shared Memory Filesystem

This commit fills in the shmfs.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of the structured memory
management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> index 8b01ebb4c30e..1dadf9b481ce 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/shmfs.rst
> @@ -3,3 +3,117 @@
[ ... ]
> +Each tmpfs inode tracks two key counters: allocated pages (resident in
> +memory) and swapped pages (evicted to swap).  These are maintained by
> +the ``shmem_charge()`` and ``shmem_uncharge()`` accounting functions,
> +which keep the inode's block usage consistent with the filesystem's mount
> +limits.

Does info->alloced track only resident pages?

Looking at mm/shmem.c, info->alloced tracks the total number of allocated
pages (both resident and swapped), as verified by the formula:
info->alloced == inode->i_mapping->nrpages + info->swapped

Additionally, do shmem_charge() and shmem_uncharge() maintain the swapped
pages counter? They do not appear to update the swapped counter, as they
pass 0 to shmem_recalc_inode(). The swapped counter is maintained
independently during swap operations.

> +Page Cache Integration
> +======================
[ ... ]
> +When a page is added to the cache for a tmpfs file, it replaces any
> +existing swap entry at that offset.  When a page is evicted by reclaim,
> +a swap entry takes its place.  Shadow entries (see
> +Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst) may also be stored to support working
> +set detection.

Are workingset shadow entries stored in the tmpfs page cache?

For shmem/tmpfs, workingset shadow entries are stored in the swap cache
(swapper_spaces xarrays), not the tmpfs page cache. The tmpfs page cache
only stores actual folios or swap entries (swp_entry_t) when a page is
swapped out. Storing shadow entries in the tmpfs page cache would cause
shmem_get_folio_gfp() to erroneously decode them as swap entries.

> +Swap Integration
> +================
> +
> +Under memory pressure, the reclaim path can evict tmpfs pages to swap just
> +like anonymous pages.  This is transparent to the filesystem — the page
> +cache slot simply transitions from holding a folio to holding a swap entry.
> +
> +When a process accesses a swapped-out tmpfs page, the page fault handler
> +reads the swap entry from the page cache, allocates a new page, reads the
> +data from swap, and inserts the page back into the cache.  This swap-in
> +path is specific to shmem and handles locking between concurrent faults
> +on the same page.

Does the page fault handler always allocate a new page and read from swap?

As implemented in shmem_swapin_folio(), the kernel first calls
swap_cache_get_folio() to check if the page is still resident in the swap
cache. If it is, the page is retrieved and inserted back into the tmpfs
page cache directly, without allocating a new page or performing disk I/O.
